title: Financiers
subtitle: Small French almond cakes made with brown butter and egg whites
excerpt: Financiers: small French tea cakes made with brown butter, almond flour, egg whites, and icing sugar. The brown butter gives a deeply nutty flavour; the egg whites and almond flour give a dense, moist crumb. The batter is baked in small moulds and ready in under 30 minutes.
sourceNotes: Financiers are small French almond cakes, their name traditionally attributed to their rectangular shape resembling gold bars, popular near the Paris Bourse in the nineteenth century. Their origins lie in the visitandine, a similar almond and egg white cake documented in French convents from the seventeenth century. Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), documents financiers. The standard recipe of beurre noisette, almond flour, egg whites, and icing sugar is widely reproduced in twentieth-century French and English-language domestic baking guides and is fully public-domain.

content.text: Financiers are one of the best things to do with leftover egg whites. The batter rests before baking, which makes them a good candidate for preparing in the morning and baking later. The longer the rest, the better the crust.
Brown the butter, make the batter, rest 1 hour

technique. Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} over medium heat, swirling until it turns golden and nutty. Pour into a bowl and cool. Combine the {{almond-flour}}, {{icing-sugar}}, and {{plain-flour}} in a large bowl. Add the {{egg-whites}} and {{vanilla-extract}} and mix until smooth. Pour in the cooled brown butter and stir to combine. Cover and rest at room temperature for at least 1 hour (or refrigerate overnight).

Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Grease a mini muffin tin generously with butter. Fill each hole three-quarters full with batter. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until the edges are deep gold, the centres spring back when pressed, and a skewer comes out clean. Cool in the tin for 5 minutes before turning out.
